div.p: Byte-level vs. Scaled Arithmetic in C Memory Management
Key Concept: In C/C++, pointer arithmetic is always relative to the size of the type pointed to. char* is used as the "Universal Byte Pointer" because sizeof(char) is guaranteed to be 1.

ul.li: void* arithmetic is a GNU extension (not standard C). In standard C, you must cast to char* for byte-level math.

div.p: Result: Each +1 advances by exactly 1 byte. This is the fundamental unit for arbitrary memory offsets.
Result: Each +1 advances by sizeof(int) = 4 bytes. The compiler automatically scales offsets by element size.

text.tspan: // Block iteration using char* for byte-level control

g.text: *block_ptr = current + block_size; // Store next block offset
current += block_size; // Jump by byte count

text.tspan: // Block iteration using char* for byte-level control

ol.li: Byte-level control: Pools use arbitrary block sizes (e.g., 64 bytes). Only char* ensures +64 is exactly 64 bytes.
Alignment safety: Arithmetic via char* allows manual enforcement of alignment boundaries (8, 16, 64-byte).
Universal Access: Casting to char* is the standard C idiom for examining the raw representation of any object.

text.tspan: // Common pattern in intrusive data structures
// 1. Math: Get byte-offset for next pointer
// 2. Access: Treat that offset as a place to store a pointer
// Common pattern in intrusive data structures
// 1. Math: Get byte-offset for next pointer
// 2. Access: Treat that offset as a place to store a pointer

ol.li: (char*)ptr: "I want to perform math in byte units."
(T**)ptr: "I want to read/write a pointer-sized value at this address."
